dataset.title: Gosling Lake, Alaska, Topography, Vegetation, Soils, Soil temperatures, and Site-Environmental Data, 2005-2021

abstract.para: This data set contains topography, permafrost soils, vegetation, soil temperatures, and site environmental data collected at Gosling Lake, central Alaska for monitoring thermokarst in burned and unburned black spruce ecosystems. For field monitoring of topography, water depths, and thaw depths over time (2005, 2014, 2021), we sampled every meter along 250 meter (m) transects oriented to cross replicate patches with differing degradation stages (Fig. SI2). For monitoring hydrology, soil, and vegetation over time, we used stratified, targeted sampling to establish three replicate permanent plots in each major ecotype (black spruce bog, spruce-birch bog, post-fire scrub bog, ericaceous bog, meadow bog, scrub fen, meadow fen) with the replicates broadly distributed along or near the transect. Vegetation was quantified by occular estimates (21 reconnassance plots or point sampling (11 permanent monitoring plots). A subset of monitoring plots had dataloggers for sampling soil temperatures.

intellectualRights.para: This work is dedicated to the public domain under the Creative Commons Universal 1.0 Public Domain Dedication. To view a copy of this dedication, visit https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/.

geographicCoverage.geographicDescription: Gosling Lake, northwest Denali National Park and Preserve

taxonomicCoverage.generalTaxonomicCoverage: All vascular plants were described to species, as well as dominant mosses and lichens

section.title: Ethical Research Practices
section.para: The research followed community standards for ethic research by having a valid scientific sampling design to test hypotheses of permafrost degradation and stabilization. The study plan was approved by the National Park Service under their Special Research Permit review process. No animals or people were the subject of the research. The analyses and results were reviewed by all the co-authors. A paper presenting the results has been peer-reviewed and accepted by a scientific journal. Data are available to the public. The research has societal value because it addresses the impacts of climate change on boreal ecosystems, and is of value to the National Park Service for their land management decisions for Denali National Park and Preserve.

description.para: The sampling was designed to quantify the effects of climate, ecological succession, and fire on permafrost formation and degradation within permafrost-affected peatlands in central Alaska. Permafrost-affected peatlands are abundant in large river basin land-scapes in the Alaska–Yukon region [11]. We targeted one of these large peatlands situated in northwestern Denali National Park and Preserve (DNPP) for study (Figure 1). Within this study area, we established two remote sensing grids and two transects to compare an unburned area (Grid 1) and an area burned in 1990 (Grid 2). After plot establishment, a portion of Grid 1 burned in 2015. The remote sensing grids were used to detect changes in ecotypes across a time series of imagery from 1954 to 2019. The burned and unburned areas, as well as the pre- and post-fire imagery allowed us to do a before–after/control–impact analysis, but without replication. The transects also were paired in burned and unburned areas to allow treatment comparisons in microtopography, hydrology, soils, and vegetation (Figure S1). Permanent monitoring plots were established in independent patches along each transect to represent the range of early to late successional vegetation.

samplingDescription.para: Field data collection included transect-level data on microtopography, and plot-level data on site environmental conditions, soil stratigraphy, soil physical and chemical properties, soil thermal regimes, and vegetation composition.

Microtopography, water levels, and thaw depths were measured every 1 m along two permanent 250 m transects (T1-unburned and T2-burned) that were established in 2005. Ground and water surface elevations were measured with an auto-level relative to permanent benchmarks (T1-100-BM, T1-154-BM, T2-000-BM, T2-168-BM, and 4 foot rebar in permafrost or below frost level in bogs) established with differential global positioning systems (DGPS). Thaw depths below ground surface were measured with a metal probe every 1 m, except where depths exceeded 3 m, then probing was done every 5 m. We also recorded the bottom of thin frost layers that we were able to punch through with the probe and the maximum probing depth in totally unfrozen soils.

Site environmental descriptions included information on observers, location, plot type, topography, geomorphology, soils, hydrology, and vegetation using an ecological land classification approach [77]. Photographs of vegetation and soil cores were taken at each site. Data were reviewed after fieldwork for consistency and completeness, and data summarizing soil and vegetation characteristics were added to the site-level data.

Soil stratigraphy was described from 10 permafrost sites (4 cores and 2 exposures with cores in 2005, 4 cores in 2014) and 10 unfrozen bogs (5 in 2005 and 5 in 2014). In unfrozen surface soils, we extracted 30 centimeters (cm) diameter shallow (40–60 cm) soil plugs with a shovel, and deeper soils with a 7 cm diameter coring tub. For frozen soils, we used a SIPRE (Snow, Ice and Permafrost Research Establishment) corer (7.5 cm diameter) and cored to 2–3 m depths. At the exposures we cleaned slumped material to expose the frozen surface and cored from the base of the exposures. Soil stra-tigraphy was described according to Natural Resources Conservation Service field sam-pling methods. Cryostructures were described according to [20]. Peat types were differ-entiated by macrostructure, color, and dominant plant macrofossils identifiable in the field with a hand lens. Soil samples were obtained every ~20 cm with additional samples taken from thin, distinctive horizons, and dimensions were measured to determine sample volume. In the field, determinations were made for wet weights, and soil pH and electrical conductivity were measured in thawed liquids or a saturated paste with a portable meter calibrated daily. In the lab, 115 samples were dried at ~60 °Celsius (C) until at a constant weight and weighed for calculating bulk density, gravimetric, and volumetric moisture. A subset of 87 samples was sent to the Colorado State Soil Laboratory for analysis of total nitrogen and carbon using a Leco TCN (Total Carbon and Nitrogen) analyzer. Organic carbon was assumed to be equal to total carbon because samples were acidic. Nine samples were analyzed for C14 age by the NSF (National Science Foundation)-Arizona AMS (Accelerator Mass Spectrometry) Laboratory and we reported the dates in conventional radiocarbon years before present (ybp).

Soil temperatures were monitored in surface (−5 cm) and deep soils (100 cm) during 2005–2008 and 2014–2021. During the first interval, soil temperatures were measured at a black spruce bog (T1-040) and a post-fire scrub bog (T2-060). During the second interval, dataloggers were added to a black spruce bog (T1-100), a post-fire scrub bog (T2-187), and two ericaceous bogs (T1-059 and T2-100). Air (T1-100) and water (T2-250, lake bot-tom) temperature sites were also added in 2014. Temperatures were recorded every 2 hours using single-channel dataloggers (Onset Corp., Hobo U22) for air/water and two-channel dataloggers (Hobo U23) for soil. For quality control, the temperature records were ex-amined for consistency with phase change temperatures (−0.2 °C) during fall freezeback and a calibration adjustment (typically less than 0.2 °C) was added if needed. For analysis, mean daily temperatures were plotted, and temperatures were summarized by year, hydrologic year (October–September, complete winter), and by thawing and freezing degree-days (base 0 °C). Thermal offsets were calculated to represent the differences between the mean annual air and surface temperatures and mean annual surface and deep temperatures [56].

Vegetation cover was sampled in one-time reconnaissance plots and permanent monitoring plots representing the range of ecotypes along the two transects. For the 21 reconnaissance plots, vegetation cover was visually estimated for each species in either 2005 or 2021. In 2014, 11 plots were permanently staked and sampled using the point sampling method used for long-term vegetation monitoring in DNPP [57]. For point sampling, we used a vertically oriented pin to determine species occurrence at 73 points per plot and noted “hits” for the first occurrence of a species within 10 height increments (0, 10, 20, 30, 50, 100, 150, 200, 300, and 400 cm), with each hit representing 1.4% cover. For points with no live plant, we recorded litter (dead plant parts) if present, or bare soil if litter was absent. Vascular taxa were identified to species level when possible, while non-vascular plants were identified to species only for dominant taxa. Voucher specimens were collected for problematic species; vascular taxa were identified by the authors, while bryophyte and lichen specimens were identified by specialists. To provide compatibility between methods, we differentiated the first hit of a species at each point (single) when there where multiple hits (multi) of that species. During data management, we assigned final taxonomic determinations for each species, and aggregated problematic (inconsistent identification) and trace species into higher taxonomic or growth form levels. We then summed the cover (1.4%) for each species (single hits only) by plot to establish total percent cover of each species. In a second step, we tabulated mean species cover by eco-type.

project.title: Collaborative Research: Effects of the Aggradation and Degradation of Ground Ice on the Evolution of Permafrost-Dominated Landscapes Under a Changing Climate
Dynamics and Consequences of Increasing Ice-wedge Degradation
Collaborative Research: The Transition Zone of Upper Permafrost: The Frontline for Permafrost Changes across Climate and Landscape Gradients

award.title: Collaborative Research: Effects of the Aggradation and Degradation of Ground Ice on the Evolution of Permafrost-Dominated Landscapes Under a Changing Climate
Dynamics and Consequences of Increasing Ice-wedge Degradation
Collaborative Research: The Transition Zone of Upper Permafrost: The Frontline for Permafrost Changes across Climate and Landscape Gradients

dataTable.entityDescription: Metadata for Soil temperature (5 cm, ~1 m depths) and air temperature data 2005-2021 for Gosling Lake Alaska thermokarst monitoring site 2022. Please refer to the attached code list file for any undefined codes.

attribute.attributeDefinition: identifier for site
Parameter type: s=soil, w=water, a=air
Treatment Code for Analysis
Year of download
Date, time, and hour of data collection
Date of data collection
Year of data collection
Month of data collection
Day according to Julian calendar
Hydrologic Year, October-September
Site-Parma-Year-Day, concatenated
Winter season, year at beginning
Depth of sensor 1, cm
Depth of sensor 2, cm
Surface temperature, deg C, as downloaded
Deep temperature, deg C, as downloaded
Air temperature, deg C, as downloaded
Air temperature, deg C, final
Surface temperature, deg C, final
Deep temperature, deg C, final
Surface temperature correction, deg. C
Deep temperature correction, deg. C
Air temperature correction, deg. C
Data quality surface temp: g=good (no or small correction<0.2 deg), f=fair (modest correction or gap filling), P=bad, unusable data (kept in for completeness)

codeDefinition.definition: bad, unusable data (kept in for completeness)

attribute.attributeDefinition: Data quality deep temp: g=good (no or small correction<0.2 deg), f=fair (modest correction or gap filling), P=bad, unusable data (kept in for completeness)

codeDefinition.definition: bad, unusable data (kept in for completeness)

attribute.attributeDefinition: Data quality air temp: g=good (no or small correction<0.2 deg), f=fair (modest correction or gap filling), P=bad, unusable data (kept in for completeness)

codeDefinition.definition: bad, unusable data (kept in for completeness)

attribute.attributeDefinition: Y=Hourly data sufficient for calculating daily degree day sums (base 0 C), N=not sufficient

codeDefinition.definition: Hourly data is sufficient for calculating daily degree day sums (base 0 C)

attribute.attributeDefinition: Surface degree day type: TDD=thawing degree days, FDD=freezing degree day sums, used to summarize hourly measurements to daily sums
Deep degree day type: TDD=thawing degree days, FDD=freezing degree day sums, used to summarize hourly measurements to daily sums
Air degree day type: TDD=thawing degree days, FDD=freezing degree day sums, used to summarize hourly measurements to daily sums
Notes pertaining to the data

dataTable.entityDescription: Vegetation cover data by species for Gosling Lake Alaska thermokarst monitoring site. Please refer to the attached code list file for any undefined codes.

attribute.attributeDefinition: SiteID with year
Site identifier code
Year of data collection
Study area code
Thermokarst stage code
Treatment code, Aggregated1
Type of plot
Multiple hit type of point sampling, S=single hit for each species per point, MS-multiple hits of each species

codeDefinition.definition: single hit for each species per point

attribute.attributeDefinition: Sampling method: O=occular estimate, PS=point sampling-single hit
Species code (first 3 of genus, first 3 for species), Aggregated for problem species
Current taxonomic name for species
Vegetation life forms
Growth form, aggregated
Percent cover for each species, 0.1 for trace species

dataTable.entityDescription: Site environmental characteristics for Gosling Lake thermokarst monitoring site. Please refer to the attached code list file for any undefined codes.

attribute.attributeDefinition: Concatenated field using Project Code and Plot ID
Site ID, often concatenated field using Project Code and Plot ID
Year of project
Date of field work
Project short name
Observer for environmental information
Soil observer last name or initials
Vegetation species and structure observer initials
Ecoregion based on Nowacki et al. 2001
Geographic landmark description
usually WGS84, units decimal degrees, but may be different if specified in ElevMethod
usually WGS84, units decimal degrees, but may be different if specified in ElevMethod
Elevation (m), Ellipsoid Ht NAD83, typical of raw gps data
Plot type: MI=Monitoring Intensive, MS+Monitoring Single Parameter, SI-Survey Soil-Veg, SR=rapid/recon soil-veg, V=Verification map, A=Air survey)
Plot size, radius or Length X Width in meters
Physical geography of the site
Macrotopographic feature of the site
Microtopographic feature of the site
Surface geomorphic unit
Thermokarst type in the site
Revised final drainage name
names for National Wetlands inventory hydrology, see REF_NWIWaterRegimeCodes or field code sheet
Final revised Soil Moisture names
Water depth above (+) or below (-) the soil surface, cm, 999 if no data
Dominant overall texture in top 40 cm, see Soil Text Dom codes
A=Alkaline; B=Basic; N=Circuneutral; S=Saline; E=brackish (see Ref_SiteChemistry for definitions)
Water pH or soil slurry pH, water used preferentially when available, 999 if no data
Water electrical conductivity (EC) in uS/cm or soil slurry EC, water used preferentially when available, uS/cm, 999 if no data
Thickness (cm) of surface organic layer, use 999 when no data
Cumulative thickness (cm) of surface and buried organic layer, use 999 when no data
Thickness (cm) of new peat formed after thermokarst, 999 if no data
Thaw depth (cm), use 999 for no data
Cryoturbation observed in soil profile: P-present or A-absent, U=Unknown
Permafrost present (either proven or highly likely): P-present or A-absent, U=Unknown
Depth (cm) to rocks, when rocks are >15% volume, use 999 for no data
Depth (cm) to top of wedge ice, 999 if no data
Depth (cm) to maximum soil observation (cm), includes frost probe depth
whether the soils were described: y/n
whether soil samples were collected: y/n
Viereck Veg class 4 Final call, see reference table REF_Site_VegCode4, ND=not determined
Name for Ecotype Vegetation Structure

Method used to estimate plant cover: O=occular estimate, OP=occular estimate photo, PS=point sampling on grid or line-single hit, PM=point sampling, multihit L-line intercept, I-incidental (for area list)
Disturbance class, natural or human induced, code or names, see REF_Site_DisturbClass

dataTable.entityDescription: Soil stratigraphy and properties for Gosling Lake thermokarst monitoring 2022, See field code sheet for variables using codes

attribute.attributeDefinition: Unique ID for site/plot and soil horizon using topdepth of layer, some Horizons may need to be split for sampleID linkage
Unique ID for site/plot and pedon
Year of sampling
Aggregated treatment code for analysis
Geomorphic stratigraphic unit, see reference table or field code sheet, nd=not determined
Thermokarst or degradation stage, see reference table or field code sheet
Depth (cm) to top of horizon
Depth (cm) to bottom of horizon
Thickness of layer (cm), Calculated field: Bottom Depth minus Top Depth
Final Revised drainage codes, see field code sheet
Texture standardized to NRCS system, combined coarse and prim and secondary fine textures, see REF_Soil_Texture or NRCS field manual
Texture, Aggregated for analysis
Peat type aggregated 1-broadly for analysis, see field code sheet
Ice structures Aggregation 1 (10-20 classes) for analyses, See Ref_Soil_Ice_Structure or field coding sheet
Peat type aggregated 1-for analysis, see PeatType on field coding sheet
Permafrost type, Aggregated to primary types for analysis, See REF_Soil_Frost_Type or field code sheet
Frost status, F=frozen or U=unfrozen
Sample type, Ave-class average (for gap filling), Vol-volumetric

Type of sampling: m=Measured, s=Split-Dup (for layers that are split and duplicated across 100 and 200 cm boundaries), e=Extended es=Ext-short (<50 cm) to get to full meter increment, el=Ext-long (>50 cm) to get to full meter increment

codeDefinition.definition: Split-Dup (for layers that are split and duplicated across 100 and 200 cm boundaries)

attribute.attributeDefinition: Type of physical sample data: Meas, Ave, Sub_AbvBlw, Sub_Nearby
Type of chem sample data: Meas, Ave, Sub_AbvBlw, Sub_Nearby
Sample ID final
Dry or Bulk Density, gm/cm3
Moisture (water) content, volumetric, %
Moisture (water) content, gravimetric, %
field pH measurement in saturated paste, usually with Oakton PCSTestr 35 multi parameter field probe
field EC measurement in saturated paste, microS/cm, usually with Oakton PCSTestr 35 multi parameter field probe
Organic Carbon, %wt, usually determined by LECO CN analyzer for acidic soils, or after acidic fumigation for alkaline soils, or by Total carbon minus Inorganic carbon
Organic Carbon Density (g/cc) for layer: Carbon density calculated from measured and substituted data, gm/cc
OC-stock (kg/m2) calculated for layer based on OCDens and DepthThick

dataTable.entityDescription: Ground and water surface elevations, and thaw depths along two transects (burned, unburned) at Gosling Lake thermokarst monitoring site, central Alaska. Please refer to the attached code list file for any undefined codes.

attribute.attributeDefinition: Transect identifier code
Date of data collection
Tripod set up label
Degradation Stage, see field coding sheet
Distance in meters
Rod Height, cm as read from autolevel
Water Surface Height, cm, as read from autolevel
Water depth, cm as measured at station
Benchmark Rod Height, cm
Surface elevation, m, calculated from benchmark and rod height
Benchmark orthometric elevation (NAD83), adjusted
Water surface elevation calculated from benchmark and rod height
Permafrost surface elevation, m, calculated from Surface elevation and thaw depth
Thaw depth measured by metal rod
Thaw depth (100-x), cm, when x=length of thaw probe above ground
Thaw depth (130-x), cm, when x=length of thaw probe above ground
Thaw depth (252-x), cm, when x=length of thaw probe above ground
Depth, m, of maximum depth of unfrozen soil observed
Elevation, m, of maximum depth of unfrozen soil observed
Depth, cm, of bottom of thin permafrost
Elevation, m, of bottom of thin permafrost
Elevation, m, of bottom of thin layer of seasonal frost

Ecotype code, black spruce bog, BWN), lowland wet spruce–birch woodland (spruce–birch bog, BWM), lowland dwarf birch–tussock bog low scrub (tussock bog, BT), lowland bog post-fire scrub (post-fire scrub bog, BSP), and lowland bog barrens (barren bog, BP). Seven ther-mokarst ecotypes included: lowland sedge-Sphagnum bog meadow (sedge-Sphagnum bog meadow, BM, young bog), lowland ericaceous bog dwarf scrub (ericaceous bog, BD, intermediate-age bog), lowland dwarf birch-ericaceous bog low scrub (dwarf birch bog, BLO, old bog), lacustrine sedge fen meadow (sedge meadow fen, BMS), lowland sweetgale fen low scrub (fen scrub, FS), lowland buckbean fen meadow (buckbean meadow fen shore, FMB), and lacustrine pond lily shallow lake (lake, LK).
Site ID for vegetation/soil plot
Notes

otherEntity.entityDescription: Soil temperature (5 cm, ~1 m depths) and air temperature data 2005-2021 for Gosling Lake Alaska thermokarst monitoring site 2022
Metadata for Vegetation cover data by species for Gosling Lake Alaska thermokarst monitoring site
Metadata for Site environmental characteristics for Gosling Lake thermokarst monitoring site. See field coding sheet for variables using codes
Coding sheet for site environmental and soil stratigraphy data
Metadata for Soil stratigraphy and properties for Gosling Lake thermokarst monitoring 2022
Metadata for Ground and water surface elevations, and thaw depths along two transects (burned, unburned) at Gosling Lake thermokarst monitoring site, central Alaska
Code definitions for abbreviations throughout the data tables. Please note that some codes in these files may be aggregated.
